narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the courage it takes to put yourself out there. Your application included your undergraduate GPA of 2.30, an LSAT score of 28, rankings in the 1st decile for both your first-year and third-year school grades, a middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 96% chance that you would fail it—far higher than what it sees in most successful applicants. This high predicted risk is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass the bar and thrive in practice. The strongest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your third-year school decile ranking of 1, well below the typical 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model a notable dip in performance compared to peers. Your first-year decile of 2, also much lower than average, reinforced this concern as the next biggest influence. Your LSAT score of 28, significantly under the dataset average of about 37, further tipped the scales toward failure, as higher scores are strongly linked to bar success in the model's view. Even your undergraduate GPA of 2.30, below the usual 3.21, and middle family income played supporting roles in heightening the risk, while your full-time status offered only a tiny nudge in the positive direction. Overall, your academic metrics stood out as weaker than those of typical students who pass the bar, placing you in a challenging comparison group. While this decision stings, especially given the barriers many Black women like you face in education, it's not the end of your journey—consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework, or exploring part-time programs or alternative law schools with holistic reviews. You have the resilience to improve these key areas and reapply stronger; many have turned similar predictions around with focused effort. We're rooting for you to rewrite this story.
